question: What is an electrical conductor?
answer: A material that lets (electric) current flow through it
accept: a material that allows the transfer of energy by electric current
a material that lets charge/electrons flow through it
misconceptions: only metals can carry energy because they are heavy

question: What is an electrical insulator?
answer: A material with very high resistance that does not let (an electric) current flow through it
accept: a material that does not allow the transfer of energy by electric current
a material that does not let charge flow through it
misconceptions: a material that stops energy existing
a material that has delocalised electrons

question: Name the family of materials that are good electrical conductors.
answer: Metals
accept: any metal
metals such as copper or gold

question: Give two examples of materials that are electrical insulators.
answer: Any two of: wood
plastic
rubber
paper
glass
accept: wood and plastic
rubber and glass
paper and wood

question: Explain, in terms of particles, why metals are electrical conductors but wood is not.
answer: Metals have delocalised electrons (electrons that are not attached / can move)
these delocalised electrons can move and carry energy through the material
wood (an insulator) has no delocalised electrons so charge cannot flow
accept: metals have free/loose electrons that move
insulators have no free electrons
misconceptions: metals are shiny so they conduct
wood is too solid for electricity to fit through
the electrons come from the battery into the metal

question: What does the word 'delocalised' mean when describing the electrons in a metal?
answer: 'Not local' - the electrons are free to move around (rather than being fixed in place)
accept: not fixed in one place
able to move around the metal

question: In a metal wire, where do the electrons that carry the energy come from?
answer: The (delocalised) electrons are already in the wire/metal
they do not come out of the battery
accept: they are in the wires the whole time
the battery just pushes the electrons that are already there
misconceptions: they come out of the battery
they are made by the power supply
new electrons are added each time the circuit is on

question: When a battery is connected to a metal loop, what does the battery actually do to the electrons?
answer: It pushes the (delocalised) electrons around the loop
so they can transfer energy from the battery to the component
accept: it makes the electrons already in the wire move around the circuit
it gives the electrons energy to carry to the component
misconceptions: it sends its own electrons into the wire
it pulls energy out of the bulb

question: A piece of wood is placed in the gap of a working bulb circuit and the bulb goes out. Explain why.
answer: Wood is an electrical insulator (has no delocalised electrons / very high resistance)
so no transfer of energy by electric current can happen through it, and the bulb cannot light
accept: wood does not let current flow so no energy reaches the bulb
wood has very high resistance so (almost) no current passes
misconceptions: the wood uses up the electricity
the wood absorbs the energy and stores it
wood blocks the light from the bulb

question: A paperclip is used to bridge the gap in a bulb circuit and the bulb lights. What does this tell you about the paperclip, and why?
answer: The paperclip is an electrical conductor
because it is a metal and has delocalised electrons that let current (transfer of energy) flow
accept: it conducts because it is metal
it lets charge flow so it is a conductor
misconceptions: the paperclip makes its own electricity
it lights because the clip is small

question: A wire has a copper core inside a plastic coating. Explain why each part is made of that material.
answer: The copper core is a conductor (metal with delocalised electrons) so it lets current flow along the wire
the plastic coating is an insulator so current cannot flow out / it is safe to touch
accept: copper carries the charge, plastic stops it escaping
metal inside to conduct, plastic outside to insulate
misconceptions: copper is used because it is cheap not because it conducts

question: Adding more batteries to a circuit that contains a wooden rod still does not light the bulb. Explain why.
answer: Wood is an insulator with very high resistance (no delocalised electrons)
so adding more batteries makes no difference - current still cannot flow through the wood
accept: more batteries cannot force current through an insulator
the wood still blocks the current no matter how many cells
misconceptions: more batteries will always make a bulb light
the extra batteries get used up by the wood
the wood becomes a conductor with enough batteries

question: What is transferred from the power supply to a component by a circuit?
answer: Energy
accept: energy by electric current
energy is transferred

question: State the one key difference between an electrical conductor and an electrical insulator in terms of electrons.
answer: Conductors have delocalised electrons (that can move)
insulators have no delocalised electrons
accept: conductors have free electrons, insulators do not
only conductors have movable electrons
misconceptions: conductors have more electrons in total

question: Insulators such as glass are said to have a 'very high resistance' rather than 'infinite resistance'. Explain what this means for the current through glass.
answer: Glass has a very high resistance so it opposes current strongly
a tiny current could in principle pass, but it is so small
that we measure/treat it as zero amps
accept: glass can technically allow a tiny current but it is effectively zero
the resistance is so high that practically no current flows
misconceptions: glass lets a normal current through
resistance has nothing to do with insulators

question: Compare the resistance of an electrical conductor with that of an electrical insulator.
answer: Conductors have a low resistance
insulators have a (very) high resistance
accept: conductors = low resistance, insulators = high resistance
misconceptions: conductors and insulators have the same resistance

question: Explain how measuring resistance can be used to decide whether a material is a conductor or an insulator.
answer: Resistance shows how much a material opposes current
a low resistance means current flows easily, so the material is a conductor
a very high resistance means almost no current flows, so the material is an insulator
accept: low resistance = conductor, high resistance = insulator
measure resistance: small value conductor, huge value insulator
misconceptions: all materials have the same resistance
high resistance means a good conductor

question: Which subatomic particle moves in the wires of a circuit to carry energy?
answer: The electron
accept: electrons
delocalised electrons
misconceptions: charge particles made by the battery

question: Which of these materials is an electrical conductor?
answer: Copper
distractors: Plastic is an insulator, not a conductor
Glass is an insulator with very high resistance
Rubber is an insulator and does not let current flow
misconceptions: thinking plastic conducts because wires have plastic coatings
thinking glass conducts because it is hard and shiny

question: Which of these is an electrical insulator?
answer: Wood
distractors: Gold is a metal and a good conductor
Iron is a metal and a conductor
Copper is a metal and a good conductor
misconceptions: thinking any solid object is an insulator
not knowing gold is a metal

question: What makes metals able to conduct electricity?
options: They contain delocalised electrons that can move
They receive electrons from the battery
They have no resistance at all
answer: They contain delocalised electrons that can move
distractors: The electrons are already in the metal; they do not come from the battery
Being heavy has nothing to do with conducting
Conductors have low resistance, not zero resistance
misconceptions: electrons come out of the battery into the metal

question: A bulb circuit works. A material is added into a gap and the bulb goes out. What is the most likely reason?
options: The added material is an insulator
The added material is a metal
answer: The added material is an insulator
distractors: A metal would let current flow and the bulb would stay lit
Adding a material does not give the battery more energy
Electrons are not used up; they keep circulating
misconceptions: electrons get used up in the bulb

question: Where do the electrons that carry energy round a metal circuit come from?
options: They are already in the wires
They come out of the battery
They are made by the switch
answer: They are already in the wires
distractors: A common error: the battery only pushes the electrons that are already there
The bulb receives energy; it does not supply the electrons
A switch only opens or closes the loop; it does not make electrons
misconceptions: the electrons come out of the battery and travel to the bulb

question: Which statement about an electrical insulator is correct?
options: It always lets a large current flow
answer: It has very high resistance
distractors: Insulators have no delocalised electrons; that describes conductors
Insulators block current, so a large current cannot flow
Zero resistance would make it a perfect conductor, not an insulator
misconceptions: insulators have free electrons like metals

question: A 1 mm wide, 30 cm long wire is made of copper and another the same size is made of glass. Which is true?
options: The copper has much lower resistance than the glass
The glass has much lower resistance than the copper
Both have about the same resistance
answer: The copper has much lower resistance than the glass
distractors: Glass is an insulator, so its resistance is far higher, not lower
Conductors and insulators have hugely different resistances
All real materials have some resistance
misconceptions: resistance is the same for all materials

question: Why is the current measured through a glass insulator treated as zero amps?
options: Its resistance is so high that any current is incredibly tiny
Glass contains no electrons at all
Glass turns the current into light
The battery refuses to push current into glass
answer: Its resistance is so high that any current is incredibly tiny
distractors: Glass still contains electrons, but they are not delocalised
Glass does not convert the current into light
The battery does push, but the huge resistance stops a measurable current

question: A current of 1 A means 1 coulomb of charge passes a point each second. How many coulombs pass through a torch circuit in 1 second if the current is 1 A?
working: 1 A = 1 C per second, so in 1 s, charge = 1 C
answer: 1 C
accept: 1 coulomb
misconceptions: thinking 1 A means 1 C is created

question: A floodlight circuit has 20 C of charge passing through it every second. What is the current?
working: 1 A = 1 C per second, so 20 C per second = 20 A
accept: 20 amps
misconceptions: thinking the answer is in coulombs
dividing 20 by the time again to get a smaller value

question: An ammeter shows that 6 C of charge passes through it in 1 second. What current does this represent?
working: 6 C in 1 s = 6 C/s = 6 A
answer: 6 A
accept: 6 amps
misconceptions: thinking current is measured in coulombs

question: A circuit carries a current of 3 A. How many coulombs of charge pass a point in the circuit in 1 second?
working: 1 A = 1 C per second, so 3 A = 3 C per second; in 1 s, 3 C pass
answer: 3 C
accept: 3 coulombs
misconceptions: thinking no charge passes because the current is small

question: A current of 4 A flows through a wire. How many coulombs of charge pass a point in 2 seconds?
working: 4 A = 4 C per second; in 2 s, charge = 4 x 2 = 8 C
answer: 8 C
accept: 8 coulombs
misconceptions: forgetting to multiply by the time and writing 4 C
dividing instead of multiplying to get 2 C

question: In 1 second, 12 C of charge pass through an ammeter. What current, in amps, does the ammeter read?
working: 12 C in 1 s = 12 C/s = 12 A
accept: 12 amps
misconceptions: thinking current depends on the wire length here

question: A switch in a circuit is left open so no charge moves. In 1 second, how many coulombs pass the ammeter?
working: With an open switch the circuit is incomplete, no charge moves, so 0 C pass in 1 s
answer: 0 C
accept: zero coulombs
none
misconceptions: thinking some charge always leaks through
thinking the battery still pushes charge with an open switch

question: A motor circuit has a current of 0 A. What is the current through it?
working: 0 C of charge passing in 1 s = 0 C/s = 0 A, meaning no current is flowing
answer: 0 A
accept: zero amps
no current
misconceptions: thinking a circuit always has some current

question: A current of 45 A flows in a circuit. How many coulombs of charge pass a point every second?
working: 1 A = 1 C per second, so 45 A = 45 C passing every second
accept: 45 coulombs
misconceptions: thinking the number must change between amps and coulombs

question: A current of 5 A flows through a wire. How many coulombs of charge pass a point in 4 seconds?
working: 5 A = 5 C per second; in 4 s, charge = 5 x 4 = 20 C
accept: 20 coulombs
misconceptions: forgetting the time and writing 5 C
adding instead of multiplying to get 9 C

question: A torch passes about 1 C of charge each second and a floodlight passes about 20 C each second. How many more coulombs pass through the floodlight than the torch in 1 second?
working: Difference = 20 C - 1 C = 19 C in 1 s
accept: 19 coulombs
misconceptions: thinking both pass the same charge

question: A motor circuit carries a current of 3 A. How many coulombs of charge pass through the ammeter in 2 seconds?
working: 3 A = 3 C per second; in 2 s, charge = 3 x 2 = 6 C
answer: 6 C
accept: 6 coulombs
misconceptions: forgetting the time and writing 3 C

question: What is electrical current?
answer: the rate of flow of charge
accept: how quickly charge flows
how quickly the charges are moving
misconceptions: the amount of charge in the wire

question: In which unit is electrical current measured?
answer: amps (A)
accept: amperes
A

question: Which piece of equipment is used to measure current, and how is it connected in a circuit?
answer: an ammeter
connected in series (in the loop)
accept: ammeter, in the main loop

question: What does potential difference tell us about a circuit?
answer: the energy transferred per unit (coulomb) of charge
accept: the energy given to each coulomb of charge
joules per coulomb
misconceptions: the rate of flow of charge
the total energy in the battery

question: In which unit is potential difference measured?
answer: volts (V)
accept: V

question: Which piece of equipment is used to measure potential difference, and how is it connected?
answer: a voltmeter
connected across (in parallel with) a component
accept: voltmeter, on either side of a component or cell

question: What is resistance?
answer: the opposition to current
accept: the slowing of the flow of charge
how much a component gets in the way of the charges
misconceptions: the rate of flow of charge

question: In which unit is resistance measured?
answer: ohms (Ω)
accept: ohms
Ω

question: Explain why we use coulombs of charge rather than counting individual electrons in a circuit.
answer: there are billions of electrons in a wire
far too many to count
accept: too many electrons to count individually
so we measure the amount of charge instead
misconceptions: there are only a few electrons
the electrons come from the battery
electrons are the same as charge units

question: Two circuits carry the same number of electrons but one has a larger current. Explain how this is possible.
answer: current depends on how fast the charges move
a larger current means the electrons are moving faster
accept: same number of charges but moving at different speeds
the faster the electrons move, the larger the current
misconceptions: the larger current has more electrons
extra electrons come out of the battery
more charge means more electrons added

question: Explain why increasing the potential difference of the power supply increases the current.
answer: more energy is transferred to the charges
so the charges move faster, giving a larger current
accept: the electrons move faster when given more energy
faster charges mean a bigger current
misconceptions: more potential difference adds more electrons
potential difference and current are the same thing

question: Explain why adding more components to a single loop makes the current smaller.
answer: each component adds more resistance / gets in the way of the charges
so the charges move more slowly and the current decreases
accept: more stuff in the way of the charges
more resistance means less current
misconceptions: more components make the current bigger

question: State two different ways you could increase the current in a circuit.
answer: increase the potential difference (e.g. add a cell)
decrease the resistance (e.g. remove a component)
accept: use a battery with a bigger potential difference
remove a component to lower resistance

question: A common mistake is to say that current and potential difference are the same thing. Explain the difference between them.
answer: current is the rate of flow of charge, measured in amps
potential difference is the energy transferred per coulomb of charge, measured in volts
accept: current = how fast charge flows; p.d. = energy per charge

question: What is a resistor designed to do in a circuit?
answer: to control / slow the flow of charge
it has no other function and gets hot when it does this
accept: to stop charges moving too quickly
to add resistance to a circuit
misconceptions: to add energy to the circuit

question: Why does a resistor get hot when current flows through it?
answer: an electron leaving the resistor has less energy than one entering it
the resistor has transferred some of the electrons' energy
accept: the charges drop off energy at the resistor
energy is transferred to the resistor
misconceptions: the current speeds up inside it

question: Describe the job of the electrons as they travel around a circuit.
answer: they pick up energy from the power supply
and drop off energy at the components
accept: they carry energy from the battery to the components
transfer energy from the supply to components
misconceptions: they are made by the battery
they get used up at the bulb
they carry the energy stored in the bulb back to the battery

question: An insulator such as glass has an extremely high resistance. Explain why we measure the current through it as zero amps.
answer: because the resistance is so high, the current that can pass is incredibly tiny
so we measure it as zero amps
accept: the current is too small to detect
high resistance means almost no charge flows
misconceptions: no current can ever pass through an insulator at all

question: A voltmeter reads 9 V across a cell. Explain what this 9 V tells you about each coulomb of charge.
answer: 9 joules of energy is transferred to each coulomb of charge
because 1 V = 1 J/C
accept: each coulomb is given 9 J of energy
9 J per coulomb

question: Which instrument measures current and how is it connected?
distractors: a voltmeter measures p.d., not current
an ammeter is correct but it is connected in series, not parallel
this describes how a voltmeter is connected, and it measures p.d.
answer: An ammeter, connected in series

question: What happens to the current in a single-loop circuit if you increase the resistance?
distractors: increasing resistance opposes the current more, so it cannot increase
more resistance does change the current, it does not stay the same
current splitting relates to parallel branches, not a single loop
answer: The current decreases
misconceptions: resistance has no effect on current

question: You remove one bulb from a single loop containing several identical bulbs. What happens to the current?
distractors: removing a component lowers the resistance, so the current rises rather than falls
fewer components means less opposition, so the current does change
the loop is still complete, so current does not stop
answer: It increases
misconceptions: fewer components always means less current
removing a bulb breaks the circuit

question: Which quantity is measured in volts?
distractors: current is measured in amps, not volts
resistance is measured in ohms, not volts
charge is measured in coulombs, not volts
answer: Potential difference

question: A circuit carries a current of 5 A. How much charge passes a point each second?
distractors: volts is the unit of potential difference, not charge
ohms is the unit of resistance, not charge
joules is the unit of energy, not charge
answer: 5 coulombs

question: Which of these correctly states the equation linking the three electrical quantities?
distractors: multiplying would mean more resistance gives more current, which is wrong
this rearrangement is upside down for resistance
this rearrangement is incorrect for potential difference
answer: current = potential difference ÷ resistance

question: A battery has a potential difference of 12 V and is connected to a bulb of resistance 3 Ω. Calculate the current.
answer: 4 A

question: A 20 V supply is connected to a component with a resistance of 4 Ω. Calculate the current.
answer: 5 A

question: A bulb has a resistance of 4 Ω and the ammeter reads a current of 3 A. Calculate the potential difference across the bulb.
working: I = V ÷ R, so V = I × R = 3 × 4 = 12 V

question: A battery has a potential difference of 24 V and the ammeter measures a current of 4 A. Calculate the resistance of the bulb.
working: I = V ÷ R, so R = V ÷ I = 24 ÷ 4 = 6 Ω
answer: 6 Ω

question: An ammeter reads that 18 C of charge passes through it in 1 second. What is the current?
working: 1 A = 1 C/s, so 18 C in 1 s = 18 C/s = 18 A

question: A circuit has a current of 6 A. How many coulombs of charge pass a point in 2 seconds?
working: 6 A = 6 C/s, so in 2 s, 6 × 2 = 12 C
misconceptions: forgetting to multiply by time, giving 6 C

question: A 2 kΩ resistor is connected to a 10 V supply. Convert the resistance to ohms and calculate the current.
Three identical 1.5 V cells are joined in a battery and connected to a 9 Ω bulb. Calculate the current. (First add the cell potential differences.)
A voltmeter reads 13.5 V across a battery and the ammeter reads 1.5 A. Calculate the resistance of the component.
answer: 9 Ω

question: A bulb of resistance 2 Ω is connected to a battery and the ammeter reads 4 A. Calculate the potential difference of the battery.
working: I = V ÷ R, so V = I × R = 4 × 2 = 8 V
answer: 8 V

question: What is a series circuit?
answer: A circuit with one loop where the components are connected one after another
accept: components connected one after another in a single loop
everything in one loop
misconceptions: a circuit with more than one loop
a circuit where each component has its own loop
a circuit where the current splits

question: What is a parallel circuit?
answer: A circuit with more than one loop, where components are connected in their own separate loops
accept: components connected in their own separate (branch) loops
a circuit with more than one independent loop
misconceptions: a circuit with only one loop
a circuit where the current is always halved

question: In a series circuit, how does the size of the current compare at different points around the loop?
answer: The current is the same everywhere (at every point in the loop)
accept: the current is the same all the way round
it does not change around the loop
misconceptions: the current is used up by each component
the current is biggest near the battery
the current splits between the components

question: In a series circuit, what happens to the potential difference of the supply across the components?
answer: It is shared between the components
accept: the p.d. is split/divided between the components
each component gets part of the supply p.d.
misconceptions: the full p.d. is across every component
the p.d. is the same across each component
the p.d. is used up and reaches zero

question: In a parallel circuit, how does the potential difference across each branch compare?
answer: The potential difference is the same across each branch (loop)
accept: each branch has the same p.d.
the p.d. across every loop is equal
misconceptions: the p.d. is shared between the branches
the p.d. is halved in each branch
the p.d. is different in every branch

question: Describe two features of a parallel circuit.
answer: There is more than one loop
each loop is independent (the p.d. is the same across each branch)
accept: components are in separate loops
each branch can work on its own
every branch has the same potential difference
misconceptions: the current always splits exactly in half
the current is the same in every part

question: Explain why adding an extra bulb to a series loop makes the current smaller.
answer: Each extra component adds more resistance
more resistance opposes the current so less current flows
accept: the extra bulb gets in the way of the charges
more resistance means a smaller current
misconceptions: the extra bulb shares the current so it is bigger
more components mean a bigger current
the current is used up by the first bulb

question: A pupil says: 'In a parallel circuit the current always splits exactly in half.' Explain why this is wrong.
answer: Each loop draws its own current depending on its resistance
so the currents in the branches are not always equal
accept: the split depends on the resistance of each branch
branches with different resistance carry different currents
misconceptions: every branch must carry the same current
the current is used up equally

question: In a parallel circuit, what happens to the currents in the separate branches where the wires join back together?
answer: The currents from the branches add together
so where the wires are shared the total current is the sum of the branch currents
accept: the branch currents combine/add up
the shared wire carries the total of the branch currents
misconceptions: only the largest branch current continues
the current halves when the wires join

question: Explain why removing one bulb from a series loop of identical bulbs makes the current larger.
answer: Removing a component reduces the resistance in the loop
with less resistance more current flows
accept: there is less stuff in the way of the charges
less resistance means a larger current
misconceptions: removing a bulb leaves more current for the others to share
the current stays the same because it is a series circuit

question: A motor and a resistor are connected in one series loop. Explain what happens to the current if the resistor is removed, and why.
answer: The current increases
removing the resistor reduces the total resistance of the loop
with less resistance opposing the charges, more current flows
accept: less in the way of the charges so they move faster
lower resistance gives a bigger current
misconceptions: the current decreases because there is less to push it
the current stays the same in series
the motor stops because the resistor shared the current

question: Two identical bulbs are connected in series. An ammeter reads 0.5 A next to the battery. What current flows through the second bulb?
answer: 0.5 A (the same)
accept: the same, 0.5 A
0.5 amps
misconceptions: 1 A because there are two bulbs
0 A because the current is used up by the first bulb

question: Why is it better to describe a parallel circuit using 'separate loops' rather than saying the current 'splits'?
answer: Each loop is independent and draws its own current depending on its resistance
saying it 'splits' suggests it always divides equally, which is a misconception
accept: the branches are not always equal so 'splitting' is misleading
each branch carries its own current set by its resistance
misconceptions: because the current really does split exactly in half
because the loops always carry the same current
because the current disappears in one of the loops

question: State one difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit in terms of the number of loops, and one difference in terms of potential difference.
answer: A series circuit has one loop while a parallel circuit has more than one loop
in series the p.d. is shared between components, in parallel the p.d. is the same across each branch
accept: series = single loop, parallel = several loops
series shares p.d.; parallel has equal p.d. on each branch
misconceptions: both have the same number of loops
p.d. behaves the same way in both
the current is shared in both

question: Two branches of a parallel circuit have different resistances. Explain why one branch carries a larger current than the other.
answer: Each branch has the same potential difference across it
the branch with lower resistance carries the larger current
accept: lower resistance allows more current for the same p.d.
more resistance in a branch gives less current
misconceptions: the current always splits equally regardless of resistance
the branch with more resistance carries more current
both branches must carry the same current

question: A battery is connected to two identical bulbs in parallel. Explain why each bulb is as bright as it would be with just one bulb on its own.
answer: The potential difference is the same across each branch (the full supply p.d.)
so each bulb has the same p.d. and current as it would on its own
accept: each branch gets the full p.d. of the supply
the loops are independent so each bulb behaves the same
misconceptions: the p.d. is shared so each bulb is dimmer
the current splits in half so each bulb is dimmer
the bulbs share the energy equally and both go dim

question: Explain why the current in a series circuit is the same everywhere, even after the charges have passed through a bulb.
answer: The same charges keep flowing round the single loop
charge is not used up by the components, only energy is transferred
so the same amount of charge passes every point each second, giving the same current
accept: the electrons are not used up, only their energy is dropped off
the rate of flow of charge is the same at every point
misconceptions: the bulb uses up some of the current
the current gets smaller after each component
new charge is added by the battery each loop

question: Compare how the current behaves in a series loop with how the potential difference behaves in that same loop.
answer: The current is the same everywhere in the loop
but the potential difference is shared between the components
accept: current stays constant; p.d. divides up
same current throughout, p.d. split between parts
misconceptions: both the current and p.d. are the same everywhere
both the current and p.d. are shared
the p.d. is constant and the current is shared

question: Explain why, when you add more components to a series loop, the current decreases. Refer to resistance in your answer.
answer: Each component gets in the way of the flowing charges
adding components increases the total resistance of the loop
more resistance opposes the current so a smaller current flows
accept: more stuff in the way means more resistance and less current
extra components raise the resistance, lowering the current
misconceptions: components add more push to the charges
the resistance falls when you add components

question: A 12 V supply is shared equally between two identical bulbs in a series circuit. What is the potential difference across each bulb?
answer: 6 V
accept: 6 volts
working: 12 V shared equally between 2 bulbs = 12 ÷ 2 = 6 V

question: In a series circuit a 9 V supply is shared equally between three identical components. What is the potential difference across each component?
answer: 3 V
accept: 3 volts
working: 9 V shared equally between 3 components = 9 ÷ 3 = 3 V

question: In a series circuit a 10 V supply is shared between two components. One component has a p.d. of 4 V across it. What is the p.d. across the other component?
answer: 6 V (the other one)
accept: 6 volts
working: p.d. shared, so other component = 10 − 4 = 6 V

question: In a parallel circuit two branches carry currents of 3 A and 4 A. What is the total current in the shared wire returning to the battery?
answer: 7 A
accept: 7 amps
working: branch currents add: 3 + 4 = 7 A

question: A parallel circuit has a total current of 8 A leaving the battery. One branch carries 5 A. What current flows in the other branch?
answer: 3 A (the other branch)
accept: 3 amps
working: branch currents add to the total: 8 − 5 = 3 A

question: Two identical bulbs are in series. The ammeter near the battery reads 2 A. What current flows through the far bulb?
answer: 2 A
accept: 2 amps
the same, 2 A
working: current is the same everywhere in a series loop, so it is 2 A

question: A bulb in a series circuit has 6 V across it and a resistance of 2 ohms. Using I = V ÷ R, calculate the current through it.
answer: 3 A through the bulb
accept: 3 amps
3 A

question: In a parallel circuit, one branch contains a 4 ohm resistor with 12 V across it. Using I = V ÷ R, calculate the current in that branch.
answer: 3 A in that branch
accept: 3 amps
3 A
working: each branch has the full p.d.: I = V ÷ R = 12 ÷ 4 = 3 A

question: In a parallel circuit each branch has the same p.d. as the 6 V supply. One branch has a resistance of 3 ohms. Using I = V ÷ R, calculate the current in that branch.
answer: 2 A in that branch
accept: 2 amps
2 A
working: branch p.d. = supply p.d. = 6 V; I = V ÷ R = 6 ÷ 3 = 2 A

question: A parallel circuit has two branches each carrying 1.5 A. What is the total current supplied by the battery?
answer: 3 A total
accept: 3 amps
working: branch currents add: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 A

question: How are components connected in a series circuit?
answer: One after another in a single loop
options: One after another in a single loop
Each in its own separate loop
In two or more independent loops
So the current always splits in half
distractors: this describes a parallel circuit, not series
this also describes a parallel circuit
this is a misconception about parallel circuits, not how series components connect
misconceptions: series components are in separate loops
series means more than one loop

question: Which statement about the current in a series circuit is correct?
answer: The current is the same at every point in the loop
options: The current is the same at every point in the loop
The current is used up by each component
The current is biggest closest to the battery
The current splits between the components
distractors: charge is not used up; only energy is transferred to components
the current does not change around a single series loop
the current does not split in a single series loop
misconceptions: current is larger near the battery

question: What happens to the current in a single series loop when you add an extra identical bulb?
answer: It gets smaller
distractors: adding components adds resistance, which reduces current, not increases it
more resistance changes the current, so it does not stay the same
current does not split in a single series loop
misconceptions: the current is unaffected by extra components
the current splits between the bulbs

question: In a parallel circuit, what is true about the potential difference across each branch?
answer: It is the same across each branch
options: It is the same across each branch
It is shared between the branches
It is halved in each branch
It is zero in all but one branch
distractors: sharing p.d. between branches describes a series rule, not parallel
the p.d. is not halved; each branch has the full supply p.d.
every branch has a p.d., it is not zero
misconceptions: the p.d. halves in each branch
only one branch has any p.d.

question: In a parallel circuit with branches of different resistance, how is the supply current shared between the branches?
answer: Each branch draws its own current depending on its resistance
options: Each branch draws its own current depending on its resistance
It always splits exactly in half
Each branch always carries the same current
All the current goes through one branch only
distractors: the split is not always equal; it depends on each branch's resistance
branches of different resistance carry different currents
current flows in every connected branch, not just one
misconceptions: the current always splits in half

question: Two branches of a parallel circuit carry 2 A and 6 A. What is the current in the shared wire where the branches meet?
answer: 8 A
distractors: 4 A is the average, but the currents add rather than average
6 A is just the larger branch; the shared wire carries the total
2 A is just the smaller branch, not the total
misconceptions: only the largest branch current continues
only the smallest branch current continues

question: Which circuit has more than one loop?
answer: A parallel circuit
distractors: a series circuit has only one loop
a single-bulb circuit is still a single loop
an incomplete circuit has no working loop at all
